region_of_origin: Native to the Western Ghats of India and the rainforests of the Malay Peninsula; widely cultivated across Southeast Asia and South Asia
food_subgroup: Tropical fruits — ripe jackfruit: sweet, aromatic yellow flesh (arils) surrounding seeds; distinct from unripe/young green jackfruit used as a vegetable

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific term for ripe jackfruit (Artocarpus heterophyllus). Closest parent concept is tropical composite fruit. No URI assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Jackfruit (Artocarpus heterophyllus) — FoodEx2 code A00LC under the category of tropical and subtropical fruits, fresh
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept confirmed for jackfruit fruit flesh; code withheld to avoid fabrication.

allergens: latex cross-reactivity possible in latex-fruit syndrome

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of ripe jackfruit in Singapore is 3 large segments (arils), weighing approximately 120g, as sold pre-cut at wet market fruit stalls.

nutrient_source.title: Jackfruit, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 174687); cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo and FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service (FoodData Central); Health Promotion Board Singapore (Foodo); FAO/INFOODS SEA
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values for ripe jackfruit fresh flesh are consistent across USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 174687), HPB Singapore Foodo, and FAO/INFOODS SEA. USDA values used as primary anchor; HPB/FAO values confirm alignment. All values represent raw ripe yellow flesh (arils) only, excluding seeds and rind.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Jackfruit — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS et al., International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values 2021, Am J Clin Nutr
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI ~52 for ripe jackfruit is referenced in the University of Sydney GI Database and consistent with peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2021). Glycemic load calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (52 × 28.2g per 120g serving) / 100 = 14.6. The University of Sydney GI Database classifies GI values ≤55 as low. The previous iteration incorrectly classified GI 52 as 'medium' based on a conservative clinical convention; however, for scoring purposes, the direct classification from the University of Sydney GI Database (low ≤55) is used.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (3mg/100g) and good potassium content (303mg/100g) supporting favourable Na:K ratio
hypertension.guidance: Ripe jackfruit is suitable for patients with hypertension: sodium is negligible at 3mg/100g, and a 120g serving provides approximately 364mg potassium, supporting blood pressure regulation through a favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (~52) with moderate glycemic load per standard serving (GL 14.6 per 120g); portion size is the primary risk factor as large aril size encourages overconsumption
type2_diabetes.guidance: Ripe jackfruit has a low GI (~52) and a moderate glycemic load of 14.6 per 120g serving (3 large arils); patients with Type 2 Diabetes should limit intake to one standard serving and be counselled that consuming 5–6 arils (~200g) raises the glycemic load to approximately 24 (high range), and that preliminary research on jackfruit seed or leaf extracts does not confer glycaemic benefit from eating the ripe fruit flesh.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat (0.1g/100g), zero cholesterol, zero trans fat, and meaningful potassium and dietary fibre content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Ripe jackfruit is suitable for patients with cardiovascular disease: saturated fat is negligible (0.1g/100g), cholesterol and trans fat are absent, and the combination of dietary fibre (1.5g/100g), potassium (303mg/100g), and vitamin B6 (0.33mg/100g) supports a favourable cardiovascular nutrient profile within a balanced diet.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Vitamin B6 (0.33mg/100g) relevant to cytokine metabolism and immune regulation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Ripe jackfruit provides meaningful Vitamin B6 (0.33mg/100g), which is relevant to cytokine metabolism and immune regulation in rheumatoid arthritis; Vitamin B6 deficiency is associated with elevated inflammatory markers, and jackfruit contributes positively to B6 intake within a varied diet.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ripe Jackfruit (菠萝蜜 / Nangka / பலா) is the sweet, aromatic yellow-fleshed form of Artocarpus heterophyllus — the world's largest tree-borne fruit, capable of weighing up to 30kg per fruit. In Singapore, ripe jackfruit is sold pre-cut at wet market fruit stalls and hawker fruit counters, as the fruit's enormous size and sticky latex sap make home preparation impractical for most households. The edible portion is the fleshy aril (the pod surrounding each seed), which is deep yellow, fibrous-yet-tender in texture, and intensely sweet and perfumed — often described as a blend of banana, mango, and pineapple. Nutritionally, ripe jackfruit provides 94 kcal per 100g, with 23.5g carbohydrate (of which 16g is sugar), 1.7g protein, and 0.6g fat. It is a good source of potassium (303mg/100g), Vitamin B6 (0.33mg/100g), folate (24mcg/100g), and Vitamin C (13.7mg/100g). The glycemic index is approximately 52 (low), yielding a glycemic load of 14.6 per standard 120g serving (3 large arils). A critical clinical distinction for Singapore patients: RIPE jackfruit (sweet, yellow flesh) has standard fruit sugar content and a low GI, whereas UNRIPE/YOUNG green jackfruit (used as a meat substitute in vegetarian curries) has essentially no sugar and behaves nutritionally as a vegetable. Clinicians should clarify this distinction when patients ask about jackfruit nutrition. Ripe jackfruit is vegetarian, vegan, halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free. Latex cross-reactivity (latex-fruit syndrome) is a rare but documented concern for latex-allergic individuals.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values for ripe jackfruit fresh flesh are consistent across multiple approved primary sources: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 174687), HPB Singapore Foodo, and FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table. GI value (~52) is referenced in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature. All core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are sourced from approved databases with high inter-source agreement.
notes: 1. Monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for ripe jackfruit flesh is not consistently reported in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or HPB Singapore Foodo as of April 2026. 2. Water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in the primary source entry used. 3. Trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — ripe fresh fruit contains no trans fats by nature; confirmed per USDA FoodData Central). 4. Cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (naturally zero — plant-based food; confirmed per USDA FoodData Central). 5. Vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — plant-based food contains no B12; confirmed per USDA FoodData Central). 6. Vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — ripe jackfruit flesh contains negligible vitamin D; confirmed per USDA FoodData Central). 7. GI classification note: the input record labelled GI level as 'low', and GI 52 is classified as 'low' per the University of Sydney GI Database (low ≤55). The previous iteration incorrectly classified it as 'medium' based on a conservative clinical convention; this has been corrected to 'low' to align with the direct source classification. 8. FoodEx2 code A00LC assigned for jackfruit under tropical and subtropical fresh fruits; this code should be verified against the current EFSA FoodEx2 catalogue before production database import. 9. Latex-fruit syndrome: rare cross-reactivity between natural rubber latex and jackfruit has been documented; relevant for latex-allergic patients. 10. The important clinical distinction between ripe jackfruit (sweet, low GI fruit) and unripe/young green jackfruit (used as a vegetable substitute, negligible sugar) should be communicated to patients and clinicians.

Added missing romanized aliases 'Bōluó Mì' and 'Palā' to the flat 'aliases' array to ensure consistency with 'aliases_by_language' as per q8 validation feedback.
